coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots as a new fluorescent nanomaterial with switchable sensing potential and its sustainable deployment for metal sensing applications
coredata.dc:creator: Berenice González-González, Reyna
Parra-Saldívar, Roberto
Ramirez-Mendoza, Ricardo A.
Iqbal, Hafiz M.N.
coredata.dc:description: Metal contaminated wastewater represents a major environmental problem of global concern. Traditional detection techniques cannot perform rapid and cost-effective on site-measurements. Recently, carbon dots (CDs) have been applied to detect metal ions in water samples; their efficient performance is attributed to the unique properties, e.g., fluorescence, water solubility, and photostability. CDs prepared from biomass and waste materials present additional advantages, e.g., lower costs and abundant and sustainable sources. This review presents biomass/waste-derived CDs to detect metal ions. Finally, possible research directions to achieve sustainability in metal sensing are suggested according to the current challenges presented in biomass/waste-derived CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanomaterials
Waste to value
Water remediation
Metal detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Recent advances on metal sensing by biomass/waste-derived CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Consejo Nacional de Ciencia y Tecnología
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Consejo Nacional de Ciencia y Tecnología

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Consejo Nacional de Ciencia y Tecnología (CONACYT) Mexico and Tecnologico de Monterrey, Mexico under Sistema Nacional de Investigadores (SNI) program awarded to Hafiz M.N. Iqbal (CVU: 735340).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of (a) “turn-on” and (b) “turn-off” fluorescence response for the detection of Fe3+ and Cd2+ as representative metal ions. Abbreviations: CDs – carbon dots, FL – fluorescence. Created with BioRender.com and extracted under premium membership.
Recent advances on metal ions sensing by biomass/waste-derived CDs.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Abbreviations: QY (quantum yield), LOD (limit of detection), NR (not reported).

head.ce:title: Carbon dots as a new fluorescent nanomaterial with switchable sensing potential and its sustainable deployment for metal sensing applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tecnologico de Monterrey, School of Engineering and Sciences, Monterrey 64849, Mexico
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Tecnologico de Monterrey, School of Engineering and Sciences, Monterrey 64849, Mexico

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Metal contaminated wastewater represents a major environmental problem of global concern. Traditional detection techniques cannot perform rapid and cost-effective on site-measurements. Recently, carbon dots (CDs) have been applied to detect metal ions in water samples; their efficient performance is attributed to the unique properties, e.g., fluorescence, water solubility, and photostability. CDs prepared from biomass and waste materials present additional advantages, e.g., lower costs and abundant and sustainable sources. This review presents biomass/waste-derived CDs to detect metal ions. Finally, possible research directions to achieve sustainability in metal sensing are suggested according to the current challenges presented in biomass/waste-derived CDs.

ce:section.ce:para: The rapid increase in industrial activities like metallurgy, textile, and papermaking have led to an extensive discharge of metal ions into the water systems causing environmental, health, and economic problems [1,2]. Traditional techniques for heavy metal detection such as inductively coupled plasma and atomic fluorescence spectroscopy are highly selective and sensitive; however, the requirement of complex/expensive equipment and specialized personnel complicate the rapid on-site detection [3]. Therefore, research efforts have been directed toward the development of inexpensive and simple sensing systems [4].

CDs, as a new fluorescent carbon nanomaterial, possess excellent optoelectrical properties useful on fluorescence-sensing applications [5]. They emerged as a potential substitute for the traditional semiconductor quantum dots due to their many advantages, such as their less toxicity, which expanded their environmental and biological applications [6]. It has been reported the development of CDs-based sensing systems able to detect different contaminants such as endocrine-disrupting chemicals [7], pharmaceutical pollutants [8], and metal ions frequently present in aquatic environments.

CDs are synthesized from two main approaches: top-down and bottom-up [9]. Several methods for the synthesis of CDs can be found in the literature; however, environmentally friendly and less expensive methods have been recently pursued [10]. Consequently, the use of waste materials as precursors of CDs to detect metal ions has been recently explored by researchers. In this review, the most recent advances in biomass/waste-derived CDs used for the detection of metal ions are summarized and discussed. Moreover, the current challenges to achieve sustainability in metal sensing through CDs are discussed to suggest possible research directions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recent advances on metal sensing by biomass/waste-derived CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The unique properties and extensive applications of CDs have led to intense research of environmentally friendly and less expensive methods. Accordingly, carbon-containing waste materials have an important role as a suitable precursor allowing the transformation of a nonvaluable material into a high-value product [11]. The synthesis/application of waste-derived CDs can be environmentally friendly and economical, while improves waste management [9]. Therefore, researchers have recently explored the use of different waste materials as CDs’ precursors in order to apply them as sensing platforms for metal contamination on wastewater [12,13]. For example, plastic waste has been used to prepare CDs with an exceptional high selectivity for Cu2+ sensing [14]. CDs from waste tea extract have reported a sensitive detection to CrO4 2− and Fe3+ ions [13]. While CDs from coconut coir presented a high selectivity to Cd2+ and Cu2+ with a limit of detection as low as 0.00018 μM and 0.00028 μM, respectively [12]. Thus, demonstrating that waste-derived CDs can be simple, eco-friendly, cost-effective, and feasible sensing systems for the detection of metal ions. CDs synthesized from biomass/waste materials are summarized in Table 1
[6,10,12–23].

Different parameters might be associated with the sensing performance of CDs. The chemical composition, size, and concentration of CDs strongly influence their photoluminescence emission, thus in their sensing properties [23,24]. Moreover, Gedda et al. [17] reported a pH-dependent behavior on the CDs’ sensing capacity of Cu2+; although they observed a quenching effect over the full range of pH, a much higher quenching effect was observed at weakly acidic conditions. Contrastingly, the Ag+ sensing capacity by CDs presented low efficiencies at acidic conditions due to hydrolysis and precipitation of Ag+ [23]. In this manner, the optimal pH is highly associated with the metal aimed to detect. On the other hand, a negligible interference effect of co-existing metal ions was evaluated on CDs synthesized by Chauhan et al. [12], demonstrating that CDs-based sensing systems can be highly efficient and selective. Therefore, the development of strategies to enhance their sensing performance and/or decontamination potential is highly needed. For instance, controlled surface functionalization, or enzyme (single and multiple) and nanozyme-based biosensing constructs, and/or decontamination of emerging pollutants using carbon-based robust materials [1,24–27]. Fig. 1 presents a schematic illustration of both fluorescence responses of CDs (turn-on and turn-off) on metal sensing applications.

Biomass and waste materials enable a low-cost synthesis of CDs while reducing the accumulation of waste materials and their consequent environmental impacts. CDs’ ability to detect metal ions through rapid and cost-effective on-site measurements represents advantageous features needed for environmental monitoring. Therefore, biomass/waste-derived carbon dots have dual role in waste management and metal contamination detection. Researchers have used a wide diversity of waste materials to synthesize CDs with an outstanding metal sensing performance, selectively detecting different metal ions contaminating water resources. Despite the evident effectiveness of biomass/waste-derived CDs, there are still challenges to solve, such as the development of multifunctional sensing systems capable of independently detecting multiple metal ions, the evaluation of CDs’ performance on complex water samples and under different conditions (pH value, temperature, reaction time, among others). Moreover, the quality enhancement of waste-derived CDs should be further explored through doping and passivation processes.

Finally, the simultaneous detection of multiple ions in water samples is frequently required. Therefore, multifunctional biomass/waste-derived CDs-sensing systems for the sustainable, selective, and independent detection of multiple metal ions are urgently needed.

Reyna Berenice González-González: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Roberto Parra-Saldívar: Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Ricardo A. Ramirez-Mendoza: Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Hafiz M.N. Iqbal: Conceptualization, Resources, Supervision, Project administration, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Consejo Nacional de Ciencia y Tecnología (CONACYT) Mexico and Tecnologico de Monterrey, Mexico under Sistema Nacional de Investigadores (SNI) program awarded to Hafiz M.N. Iqbal (CVU: 735340).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Controllable functionalization of carbon dots as fluorescent sensors for independent Cr(VI), Fe(III) and Cu(II) ions detection
J. Photochem. Photobiol. A Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Zheng, S. Ren, L. Wang, et al. 2021. Controllable functionalization of carbon dots as fluorescent sensors for independent Cr(Ⅵ), Fe(III) and Cu(II) ions detection. J. Photochem. Photobiol. A Chem. 417, 113359.
